primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic peripheral arterial disease (intermittent claudication) due to severe atherosclerotic hyperlipidaemia (probable familial hypercholesterolaemia)

comorbidities.name: Widespread atherosclerosis with bilateral carotid bruits

pathophysiology_summary: Severe, longstanding hyperlipidaemia — signalled by the physical stigmata of tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma and corneal arcus (a constellation strongly suggestive of familial hypercholesterolaemia) — has driven widespread atherosclerotic plaque, including in the lower-limb arteries. Flow-limiting stenoses cause exertional muscle ischaemia: when the calf muscles demand more oxygen during walking the narrowed vessels cannot deliver it, producing reproducible cramping pain (intermittent claudication) that resolves with rest as demand falls. The 6-month course with acute worsening over a week reflects progressing stenosis. The limb is currently viable at rest (no rest pain, no pallor/coldness, pulses palpable proximally), distinguishing chronic claudication from acute or critical limb ischaemia, but the absent distal pulses and bruits mark a high systemic atherosclerotic burden and a very high risk of myocardial infarction and stroke. Definitive management is aggressive lipid-lowering (high-intensity statin), antiplatelet therapy, a structured/supervised walking program to promote collateral vessel growth, risk-factor modification, and vascular assessment (ABI, duplex) — none of which can be delivered or the diagnosis confirmed via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: intermittent claudication from peripheral arterial disease
differential.evidence_basis: Reproducible exertional calf pain relieved by rest is the textbook claudication pattern.
Calf pain raises DVT, but DVT pain is typically constant with unilateral swelling/erythema, not exertional.
Can mimic exertional leg pain, but is relieved by spinal flexion/sitting rather than simply standing still.
Acutely worsening symptoms mandate active exclusion of a limb-threatening arterial occlusion; the exam that excludes it cannot be done remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, claudication leads but DVT and acute/critical limb ischaemia are the important-not-to-miss distractors that must be screened.

differential.diagnosis: intermittent claudication from peripheral arterial disease
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional, reproducible, rest-relieved pain relieved by standing (not flexion) fits arterial claudication.
No constant pain, no unilateral swelling, erythema or tender cord; pain is strictly exertional.
Relieved by standing still rather than sitting/lumbar flexion, and no back or neurological symptoms.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History alone excludes DVT and neurogenic claudication; acute/critical limb ischaemia remains the live safety question because it needs examination to exclude.

differential.evidence_basis: No rest pain, no night pain, foot warm and normal colour — the 6 Ps of acute limb ischaemia are absent, so the limb is viable.
Currently screened negative on history, but cannot be definitively excluded by telehealth (no pulse exam / ABI) — remains the escalation trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even with a reassuring red-flag screen, telehealth cannot palpate pulses or perform an ABI; an urgent same-day in-person assessment is the honest tier.

differential.diagnosis: symptomatic peripheral arterial disease driven by severe hyperlipidaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Claudication pattern plus stigmata of severe lipid disorder (xanthomas/xanthelasma/arcus) point to systemic atherosclerosis; confirmation needs ABI, duplex and fasting lipids.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final diagnosis is presumptive pending vascular assessment and fasting bloods; the AI's job is recognition, urgent in-person referral and safety-netting, not remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: Acutely worsening claudication over one week on a 6-month background — progressing arterial stenosis that can herald critical limb ischaemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid deterioration signals a tightening arterial supply and warrants urgent, not routine, vascular assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to critical limb ischaemia, rest pain, tissue loss and potential limb loss if the trajectory is not acted on.

red_flags.description: Features of acute limb ischaemia (the 6 Ps: rest pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia) — currently screened negative
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their appearance converts a stable chronic problem into a surgical emergency; the limb is currently viable but this must be actively safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed acute arterial occlusion causes irreversible ischaemia within hours and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Absent bilateral dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses with weak femoral pulses (clinician examination) — high systemic atherosclerotic burden
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective evidence of significant peripheral arterial disease and, with carotid bruits, of widespread atherosclerosis and very high cardiovascular event risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating the systemic burden risks missing the high near-term risk of myocardial infarction and stroke.

red_flags.description: Physical stigmata of severe familial lipid disorder — tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma, corneal arcus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mark a lifelong severe lipid burden (probable familial hypercholesterolaemia) demanding high-intensity statin therapy and cascade family screening; xanthelasma and arcus are video-observable, tendon xanthomas require palpation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing familial hypercholesterolaemia leaves the patient and untested relatives at very high premature cardiovascular risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the chronic, usually asymptomatic label of high cholesterol and treating this as a routine lipid problem, missing that new/worsening claudication needs urgent vascular assessment.
Mistaking exertional calf pain for a DVT and reaching for anticoagulation or a venous ultrasound pathway — the pattern is arterial, not venous.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a viable limb
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured that the foot is warm and pink at rest and under-triaging — telehealth cannot palpate pulses or perform an ABI, so acute limb ischaemia cannot be excluded remotely.
Confusing arterial claudication with spinal stenosis; the discriminator is relief by simply standing still (arterial) versus needing to sit or flex the spine (neurogenic).

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Anticipated < 0.9 (reduced), consistent with peripheral arterial disease — not obtainable via telehealth
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms and grades PAD; a very low ABI indicates severe disease and guides revascularisation urgency.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High-risk category (clinically evident atherosclerotic disease plus severe hyperlipidaemia)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Established atherosclerotic disease places the patient in the highest treatment category — intensive lipid lowering and antiplatelet therapy indicated.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses
Auscultation of carotid and femoral bruits
Fasting lipid profile, HbA1c and 12-lead ECG
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP or urgent care) for pulse examination and to exclude acute/critical limb ischaemia, with an urgent vascular referral for ABI and vascular surgery review; safety-net explicitly for the 6 Ps of acute limb ischaemia, escalating to the emergency department / 000 if they appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis of claudication is well supported by history, but the real risk is UNDER-action — anchoring on the benign lipid label and managing it as routine, or falsely excluding acute limb ischaemia that only an in-person pulse/ABI exam can rule out.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC/ESVS Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Peripheral Arterial Diseases (2017)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Intermittent claudication is managed with risk-factor control, antiplatelet and statin therapy and supervised exercise; acute limb ischaemia (the 6 Ps) is a surgical emergency requiring immediate referral.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / NHFA-CSANZ cardiovascular risk and lipid management guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Physical stigmata of familial hypercholesterolaemia warrant high-intensity statin therapy, absolute CVD risk assessment and cascade family screening.
